tierDefinitions.core: Referenced by ≥70% of use cases. Expected for most methods.
tierDefinitions.common: Referenced by 40-69% of use cases. Important for broad adoption.
tierDefinitions.specialized: Referenced by <40% of use cases. Depends on target use case.

requirements.name: Decentralization
requirements.description: DIDs should eliminate the requirement for centralized authorities or single points of failure in identifier management.
requirements.question: Does your method operate without a single centralized authority that can unilaterally control, revoke, or censor identifiers?

requirements.name: Control
requirements.description: DID controllers must be able to control their identifiers without requiring permission from external authorities.
requirements.question: Can the DID controller update, rotate keys, and manage their identifier without needing approval from any third party?

requirements.name: Privacy
requirements.description: Entity owners must be able to control the privacy of their information, including the amount and type of PII exposed.
requirements.question: Does your method allow the DID controller to minimize personal information exposure? Can they use the DID without revealing their real-world identity?

requirements.name: Security
requirements.description: DIDs must provide sufficient security for requesting parties to depend on DID Documents for their required level of assurance.
requirements.question: Does your method provide cryptographic security guarantees (integrity, authenticity) that relying parties can trust?

requirements.name: Proof-based
requirements.description: DID Documents must provide cryptographic proof mechanisms (e.g., digital signatures) to authenticate DID controllers.
requirements.question: Does your DID Document include verification methods (public keys, proof types) that enable cryptographic authentication?

requirements.name: Persistence
requirements.description: DIDs must be persistent enough to be long-lived so they can serve as long-term identifiers.
requirements.question: Will your DIDs continue to resolve and remain valid over years/decades? What happens if infrastructure changes?

requirements.name: Cryptographic Verifiability
requirements.description: It must be possible to cryptographically verify the DID Controller, including verification of the claims in the DID Document.
requirements.question: Can a verifier cryptographically confirm that the entity presenting the DID is its legitimate controller?

requirements.name: Cryptographic Extensibility
requirements.description: The DID infrastructure must not be limited to a single cryptographic suite or algorithm.
requirements.question: Does your method support multiple cryptographic algorithms? Can new algorithms be added without changing the method specification?

requirements.name: Discoverability
requirements.description: DIDs must be designed so authorized parties can discover them and learn more about the entities they represent.
requirements.question: How can someone discover your DID? Is there a well-known resolution path, registry, or lookup mechanism?

requirements.name: Interoperability
requirements.description: DIDs should be broadly interoperable with existing tools and software systems.
requirements.question: Does your method work with existing DID toolkits (Veramo, SpruceID, etc.)? Is there a Universal Resolver driver?

requirements.name: Portability
requirements.description: DIDs should be system- and network-independent, enabling portability across platforms.
requirements.question: Can a DID created with your method be migrated to a different infrastructure without changing the identifier?

requirements.name: Inter-jurisdictional
requirements.description: DIDs must not depend on the legal jurisdiction in which they are issued. Valid anywhere without loss of fidelity.
requirements.question: Does your method work globally without requiring jurisdiction-specific modifications or approvals?

requirements.name: Key Rotation without ID Change
requirements.description: It must be possible to rotate cryptographic keys without changing the DID.
requirements.question: Can the DID controller rotate their keys while keeping the same DID identifier?

requirements.name: Uniqueness
requirements.description: Every DID must be globally unique, with negligible probability of collision.
requirements.question: How does your method guarantee global uniqueness? What is the collision probability?

requirements.name: Service Endpoint Support
requirements.description: The infrastructure must enable the discovery of service endpoints for a given entity.
requirements.question: Does your DID Document support service endpoints? Can relying parties discover services (messaging, APIs, etc.) via the DID?

requirements.name: Composability
requirements.description: DIDs should be combinable with other technologies to form more complex applications.
requirements.question: Can your method compose with VCs, DIDComm, or other DID methods? Does it extend or call other methods?

requirements.name: Equivalence
requirements.description: It must be possible to describe equivalence relationships between two DIDs.
requirements.question: Does your method support alsoKnownAs or similar properties to link equivalent identifiers?

requirements.name: Legal Compatibility
requirements.description: The system must support the legal rights and regulatory obligations of its users.
requirements.question: Is your method compatible with regulatory frameworks (eIDAS, GDPR, etc.)? Can it support legally binding transactions?

requirements.name: Metadata
requirements.description: The infrastructure must support the association of metadata with an identifier.
requirements.question: Does your resolution process return metadata (created, updated, deactivated timestamps, version info)?

requirements.name: DID Resolution
requirements.description: The infrastructure must support the resolution of a DID to a DID Document.
requirements.question: How is your DID resolved to a DID Document? What is the resolution protocol and expected latency?

requirements.name: CRUD Operations
requirements.description: DID methods must define how CRUD operations are performed for their specific method.
requirements.question: Does your spec clearly define Create, Read, Update, and Deactivate operations?

requirements.name: Deactivation
requirements.description: It must be possible to deactivate a DID, after which no further updates or authentications should be possible.
requirements.question: Can a DID be permanently deactivated? What does the resolution response look like after deactivation?
